title: What do the angle brackets actually buy you?

mcq.question_md: Compared to a raw `ArrayList`, what's the main benefit of declaring `ArrayList<String>`?
options: The list runs faster because Java knows the element type
Wrong-type elements are rejected at compile time, and get() needs no cast
The list uses less memory per element
It allows storing primitives like int directly
mcq.explanation_md: Generics exist for **compile-time type safety**: `list.add(42)` on an `ArrayList<String>` fails before the program ever runs, and `get()` hands back a `String` with no cast. The tempting answer is speed — surely telling Java the type helps performance? It doesn't: generics are erased at compile time (*type erasure*), so at runtime a raw list and a generic list are the same class doing the same work. Memory is unchanged for the same reason, and primitives are still banned — you need `Integer`, not `int`. Generics move bugs from runtime to compile time; that's the whole (very valuable) trick.
